RONDO FORM | 788 Broadway | MARBLE SPIRE | INSPIRED BY HEAVEN AND HELL BY KANYE WEST

MLA BIBLIOGRAPHY & LYRICAL PHRASE EXPLANATIONS

[Verse 1 – A: The Anchor]

MENTIONED AS: "Grace Church, at 10th Street" — "Seven-eighty-eight Broadway, bend where the road shifts west" → Drawn from: "There is a reason for the sudden bend in Broadway at Tenth Street, close by Grace Church. The Bowery Lane, which is now Fourth Avenue, curved in passing through what is now Union Square until, at the line of the present Seventeenth Street it turned and took a direct course north and was from thereon called the Bloomingdale Road." Hemftreet. (1899) Nooks and Corner of Old New York, p. 175.

MENTIONED AS: "Grace Church" — "Pinnacle of alabaster, crystal of rose quartz blessed" → Directly from: "a 'pinnacle of alabaster,' as the civic chronicler George Templeton Strong wrote in 1870, or, on a wintry twilight, a 'great crystal of rose quartz.'" Dunlap & Vechoine. (2001) Glory in Gotham, p. 51.

MENTIONED AS: "Grace Church" — "White marble Gothic spire, Sing Sing convicts cut the stone" → Drawn from: "This Gothic Revival Episcopal church, designed in 1843 by James Renwick Jr, was made of marble quarried by prisoners at 'Sing Sing,' the state penitentiary in the town of Ossining, 30 miles up the Hudson River." St Louis. (2016) Lonely Planet New York City, p. 136.

MENTIONED AS: "Grace Church (L)" — "Renwick twenty-five years old, an engineer alone" → Drawn from: "the young architect James Renwick Jr." and "Renwick was only twenty-five when he designed Grace, and he was at once in love with Pugin and unbound by Gothic Revival dogma." Tauranac. (1979) Essential New York, p. 30.

MENTIONED AS: "Grace Church (LM) Episcopal" — "Studied copybooks of Pugin, never seen a Gothic church" → Drawn from: "his only training had been in engineering, and he'd never seen a Gothic church." Bergman. (2001) The Spiritual Traveler New York City, p. 130.

MENTIONED AS: "Grace Church Complex" — "French and English details mixed, flamboyant tracery on the perch" → Drawn from: "The rose window in the west façade recalls the curvilinear tracery of the flamboyant style; the windows and buttresses are mixtures of French and English details; the openwork spire is a typical German form." Spielvogel. (2016) The Landmarks of New York, p. 157.

[Verse 2 – B: The Subject]

MENTIONED AS: "800-804 Broadway at East 10th Street" — "Lavinia Warren, General Tom Thumb, eighteen-sixty-three" → Directly from: "On February 10, 1863, the dwarfs Lavinia Warren, 'The Little Queen of Beauty,' and Charles S. Stratton, 'General Tom Thumb,' were married at Grace Church." Holmes. (2004) 100 New Yorker, p. 19.

MENTIONED AS: "800-804 Broadway at East 10th Street" — "Brady made the cartes-de-visite, sold on every tour for free" → Drawn from: "Mathew Brady produced cartes-de-visite photographs of the bride and groom, which the couple sold during world tours." Holmes. (2004) 100 New Yorker, p. 19.

MENTIONED AS: "800-804 Broadway at East 10th Street" — "Broadway shut down, thousands clogged the streets to see" → Directly from: "Parts of Broadway shut down, as thousands more clogged the streets to catch a glimpse of the bride and groom and their glamorous guests." Holmes. (2004) 100 New Yorker, p. 19.

MENTIONED AS: "Grace Church" — "'True we are little, perfect in our littleness,' the plea" → Directly from: "'It is true we are little, but we are as God made us, perfect in our littleness!'" Dunlap & Vechoine. (2001) Glory in Gotham, p. 51.

MENTIONED AS: "800 Broadway" — "Upper ten, the bon ton, crème de la crème inside" → Directly from: "the elite, the creme de la creme, the upper ten, the bon ton, the select few, the very first families of the city—nay of the country." Bunyan. (1990) All Around the Town, p. 88.

MENTIONED AS: "800 Broadway" — "Lincoln said, 'the long and short of it,' with pride" → Directly from: "Abraham Lincoln, who remarked on their respective heights, 'God likes to do funny things, here you have the long and the short of it.'" Bunyan. (1990) All Around the Town, p. 88.

[Chorus – A: Return to Anchor]

MENTIONED AS: "Grace Church" — "Marble spire, marble spire — seen from City Hall to here" → Drawn from: "A bend in Broadway gives Grace Church a special spot in the cityscape, allowing its marble Gothic spire to be seen from as far away as City Hall Park." Dunlap & Vechoine. (2001) Glory in Gotham, p. 51.

MENTIONED AS: "Grace Church (LM) Episcopal" — "Lath and plaster vaulting, but the Gothic spirit clear" → Directly from: "The elaborately ribbed vaulting inside is of lath and plaster, not stone." Bergman. (2001) The Spiritual Traveler New York City, p. 130.

MENTIONED AS: "Grace Church (Episcopal) Complex" — "Tower dominates the vista, Broadway bending at the light" → Directly from: "At the bend of Broadway, its tower dominates the vista from the south." Postal & Dolkart. (2009) Guide to New York City Landmarks, p. 67.

MENTIONED AS: "Grace Church" — "Marble spire, marble spire — burning through the night" → Drawn from: "Be sure to find the bust of a bearded J Renwick, Jr.... look up at the decorative 25-foot marble steeple from 1883, which replaced the original wooden spire. It can be seen lighting up the evening sky for miles around." Cook. (2001) Sacred Havens, p. 37.

[Verse 3 – C: Secondary Context]

MENTIONED AS: "Grace Church (LM) Episcopal" — "Sexton Isaac Brown ran funerals entertaining as he could" → Directly from: "always choreographed by sexton Isaac Brown" and "he insisted that 'we manage to make our funerals as entertaining as possible.'" Bergman. (2001) The Spiritual Traveler New York City, p. 130.

MENTIONED AS: "Grace Church (LM) Episcopal" — "Lent was 'horridly dull,' but burials were good" → Directly from: "although he admitted that Lent was 'horridly dull,' he insisted that 'we manage to make our funerals as entertaining as possible.'" Bergman. (2001) The Spiritual Traveler New York City, p. 130.

MENTIONED AS: "Fleischmann's Vienna Model Bakery" — "Breadline born on the corner, Vienna Bakery gave away" → Directly from: "The term 'breadline' originated on this corner as the needy lined up after hours to receive free baked goods from the charitable owners." Bunyan. (1990) All Around the Town, p. 87.

MENTIONED AS: "Fleischmann's Vienna Model Bakery" — "Needy lined up after hours at the close of day" → Directly from: "this was once a bake shop popular with women shoppers in the heyday of 'Ladies Mile.' The term 'breadline' originated on this corner as the needy lined up after hours to receive free baked goods from the charitable owners." Bunyan. (1990) All Around the Town, p. 87.

MENTIONED AS: "Vienna Bakery" — "Now a leafy churchyard where the bake shop used to stand" → Drawn from: "Until 1905 the Vienna Bakery stood on the corner lot to the south. Each evening it gave away products unsold during the day (by some accounts the first 'bread line'), but its purchase by the church guaranteed protection of this green space." Bergman. (2001) The Spiritual Traveler New York City, p. 130.

MENTIONED AS: "Grace Church" — "Year-round shelter, six homeless guests — grace across the land" → Directly from: "It would probably startle Sexton Brown to know that his church now prides itself in operating a year-round shelter for six homeless 'guests.'" Dunlap & Vechoine. (2001) Glory in Gotham, p. 51.

[Outro – A: Final Synthesis]

MENTIONED AS: "Grace Church" — "From Rector Street cornerstone, eighteen-oh-six, set in the wall" → Directly from: "Set in a wall of Grace Church is the 1806 foundation stone from the first sanctuary, which stood at Broadway and Rector Street." Dunlap & Vechoine. (2001) Glory in Gotham, p. 51.

MENTIONED AS: "Grace Church (L)" — "To the chancel Heins and La Farge extended, standing tall" → Drawn from: "a year later the chancel was extended to accommodate the boys' choir. Heins & La Farge designed the extension, the first work done on the church until then by anyone but Renwick, who had died in 1895." Tauranac. (1979) Essential New York, p. 30.

MENTIONED AS: "Browere's Busts of Distinguished Americans" — "Fleischmann's gone, Browere's busts forgotten in the room" → Drawn from: "These facts, suggested by the modest sign, 'Browere's Busts of Distinguished Americans,' on the front of the building No. 788 Broadway, were told me some twenty years ago by an old New Yorker.... Twenty-three busts were ranged around the sides." Todd. (1907) Sketches of old Times and Places In Both The State And The City, p. 18.

MENTIONED AS: "Grace Church" — "Seven-eighty-eight Broadway — marble spire above the gloom" → Drawn from: "a 'pinnacle of alabaster,' as the civic chronicler George Templeton Strong wrote in 1870, or, on a wintry twilight, a 'great crystal of rose quartz.'" Dunlap & Vechoine. (2001) Glory in Gotham, p. 51.
